metric_name: PK Response 1: data warehouse only good for past business
metric_value: Right topic, wrong answer. The data warehouse approach is only good for looking at past business — real-time warehousing is cutting edge. Key EAI points are buy vs. build. Crossworlds offers handful of application-to-application solutions; Oberon et al offer toolkits. Real alternative is rolling your own with object-broker-based toolkit, because of intricate proprietary details of every enterprise's processes. Dark horse: ERP vendors moving toward CIS/warehousing/vertical depth.

metric_name: PK Response 2: Y2K-unprepared narrative is whistling in dark
metric_value: Arguing the world is unprepared for Y2K is whistling in the dark. This topic needs scenarios with depth: when will IS realize the problem; will remediation be in 2000 or 1999; what pressure/event will produce IS epiphany; how can IS test ISV applications; will testing+correcting make tangible difference vs. fail-and-remediate-in-2000?
notes: Kastner verbatim — proved correct: most fixed in 1999

metric_value: Very interesting scenario — line business units cut off from IS support must work with existing LANs, Microsoft Office97, NT and Unix servers. Forget Office 2000 (delayed Q2) or Windows 2000. Three contingency approaches: (a) Microsoft VBA + Office97 + Access + Outlook for workflow; (b) Lotus Notes shops build around Notes + Office97; (c) Intranet toolset (need more research)

metric_name: PK Response 4: Intel banner year — agreement with Intel
metric_value: Agree and have formally told Intel that this is our scenario.

(a) Let's pick ten areas where new technology is driving new entrants and where the big guys aren't yet playing; (b) let's name some likely (and unlikely) merger/acquisitions for 1999. And don't forget that stock price (e.g., Internet-stock price mania) is not the measure of market success.

Tie this no-brainer to the mainframe market. Amdahl, Hitachi, and IBM are equivalent, so IT gets very good at negotiating best terms for 18-month-to-4-year period. Hidden issue: whether IS is prepared to manage shared (currently separate) telecomm facilities and shared bandwidth/QoS. I say not ready.

metric_name: PK Response 7: Sun ascendancy as inevitable
metric_value: Sun's ascendancy is inevitable. We should focus our analysis on where Sun is really good and where buyers should look elsewhere/where Sun can improve. Secondary analysis (by Sloane and Fournier) is about evolution of Sun's software strategy, especially server-side Internet-to-database stuff.

metric_name: PK Response 8: Windows 2000 slow adoption + Office 2000 delay
metric_value: We have a new research report on this subject. Note also that Office 2000 will arrive later than expected Q1 and Office represents 40% of Microsoft's profits. Key feature of Office 2000 is ability to move into and out of HTML — great for Intranet posting.
Mr. Sakakeeny's group will tell us about next-generation Intranet-based applications development while Mr. Alschuler will ponder building ever-more-powerful web commerce sites.

Security used to be one program on the mainframe. Now, security issues are rampant as distributed systems create enormous complexity. Further, TCP/IP, for all its benefits, opens enormous holes in any LAN or WAN environment. Is this a problem solved by Line of Business Managers or by the CIO? Can it be solved?

metric_name: PK Extension 11: 1999 year of the object
metric_value: After ten years in the marketplace, object-oriented development is most commonly found in supplier's software products, not in IT applications. In 1999, leading ERP suppliers and IBM's San Francisco project will open a new era of objects for practical business use.
notes: Kastner-added trend — partial validation; OO became ubiquitous in supplier products

metric_name: PK Extension 12: computing as utility service via ISPs
metric_value: What are the dimensions, strategies, scenarios and segments of the development of the ISP business? Are they just toll-takers on the Internet highway, or is there a long-term, sustainable business here? Consolidation, hot niches, key products and services aimed at ISPs. And, will 1999 be the year of IP telephony?
notes: Kastner-added trend — DIRECTLY foreshadowed cloud computing (AWS 2006)

metric_value: Peter Kastner annotating JRL trend list with 'PK Response' style — Aberdeen Group Research Director, 1999
notes: Filename TRENDS-1-14 with PK responses; matches IBM-MS NT Clusters 1999-05 attribution

metric_name: JRL Trend 4: Intel banner year via SME PC replacement
metric_value: JRL: small-to-medium enterprises planning to replace PCs and LAN servers in mid-1999 to meet Y2K issues + replace DOS/Windows 3.1/Windows 95 desktops + outdated servers. SME decision makers do not yet see they could all be trying to squeeze each other out at the same time in 3Q99.

metric_name: JRL Trend 7: Sun enterprise data center share gain
metric_value: JRL: Data center managers implementing web-based applications see Sun as one of few large beacons of intellectual content from supplier community. While HP has higher-end servers and IBM better services, Sun's enterprise application architectures and JAVA championing put it in forefront
